did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper implemented and evaluated hybrid deep learning models combining EfficientNet (B0-B3) and Vision Transformer (ViT) architectures for skin cancer classification using the HAM10000 dataset. The study utilized convolutional neural networks and transformer-based models to extract features, fuse them, and classify skin lesions into 7 distinct categories.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental results from implementing and evaluating hybrid deep learning models (EfficientNet and ViT) on the HAM10000 dataset for skin cancer detection. It includes specific methodology sections (Data Processing, Implemented Models), quantitative results (accuracy, F1 scores), and a conclusion based on the authors' own experiments, rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Skin Disease Detection : Implementing Hybrid Classification Model to Detect Skin Cancer
journal: 2025 8th International Conference on Information and Communications Technology (ICOIACT)
brief_summary: This study addresses skin cancer detection by implementing and evaluating hybrid deep learning models that combine EfficientNet (B0-B3) and Vision Transformer (ViT) architectures. The models were trained and tested on the HAM10000 dataset, which contains over 10,000 skin lesion images categorized into seven distinct classes. The methodology involved feature extraction from both CNN and transformer backbones, followed by feature fusion and classification using a complete connectivity layer. Evaluation metrics included accuracy, macro-averaged F1 score, and weighted-averaged F1 score. The results indicated that the EfficientNetB3 standalone model and the EfficientNetB1 + ViT hybrid model achieved the highest accuracy of 0.89%. The study concluded that there was no significant performance improvement from the hybrid approach compared to the strong standalone EfficientNetB3 model under the current configuration.

all_task_types.target: 7 distinct skin disease/lesion classes (akiec, bcc, bkl, df, mel, nv, vasc)
all_task_types.details: The paper implements hybrid deep learning models (EfficientNet and ViT) to classify skin lesions into 7 distinct diagnostic categories using the HAM10000 dataset. The output is a discrete class label, evaluated via accuracy and F1 scores. The model uses a softmax output layer to generate classification logits indicating the likelihood of an image matching one of the specified skin disease types.

datasets_used.name: Skin Cancer MNIST: HAM10000
datasets_used.description: A public dataset sourced from Kaggle containing over 10,000 images of skin lesions along with 5 CSV files containing metadata (lesion_id, image_id, dx, dx_type, age, sex, localization). The images are pictures of visible skin abnormalities.

use.training: Used to train the EfficientNet (B0-B3) and Vision Transformer (ViT) models, as well as the hybrid models, after preprocessing (resizing, augmentation, normalization).
use.testing: Used to test the classification models and evaluate performance metrics (accuracy, F1 scores, AUC).

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance comparisons between models were based on descriptive statistics (accuracy, F1 scores, AUC) without statistical significance testing.

HAM10000.notes: EfficientNetB3 standalone model and EfficientNetB1 + ViT hybrid model achieved the highest accuracy of 0.89. EfficientNetB0 performed the worst with 0.85 accuracy. The difference in outcomes between models was tiny, specifically 0.02% to 0.04% for accuracy, 0.03% to 0.07% for macro-averaged F1 score, and 0.02% to 0.04% for weighted-averaged F1 score. No significant improvement was observed from the hybrid approach compared to the strong standalone EfficientNetB3 model. For EfficientNetB1 + ViT, Vascular Lesions and Dermatofibroma achieved perfect AUC scores, while Melanoma had the lowest AUC score of 0.95. For EfficientNetB3, Dermatofibroma achieved a perfect AUC score, Vascular Lesions had an AUC of 0.99, and Melanoma had the lowest AUC score of 0.94.

architecture_used.main_model: Hybrid model combining EfficientNet (B0-B3) and Vision Transformer (ViT-B/16); Standalone EfficientNet (B0-B3) and standalone ViT also evaluated.
architecture_used.pretraining: EfficientNet models pretrained on ImageNet (implied by normalization values [0.485, 0.456, 0.406] and [0.229, 0.224, 0.225]); ViT pretrained on large datasets (mentioned as a general characteristic of ViT).

architecture_details.efficientnet_backbone: EfficientNetB0, B1, B2, B3. Uses compound scaling of depth, width, and resolution. Extracts local features (texture, color, edges).
architecture_details.vit_backbone: ViT-B/16. Breaks input into patches, linearly projects to embeddings, adds positional encoding, processes via standard transformer encoder. Extracts global features/context.
architecture_details.fusion_method: Concatenation of feature vectors from EfficientNet and ViT.
architecture_details.fusion_layers: Concatenated features passed through connected layers with batch normalization, ReLU activation, and dropout.
architecture_details.dense_layer: Fully connected layer with 512 units, followed by ReLU activation and dropout for regularization.
architecture_details.training_strategy: Early layers of both backbones frozen; later layers and fusion module fine-tuned.

training_details.preprocessing: Resized to 224x224, data augmentation (random horizontal/vertical flip, random rotation, color jittering), normalization using ImageNet mean [0.485, 0.456, 0.406] and std [0.229, 0.224, 0.225], converted to PyTorch tensors.

classification_or_output_head.output: Classification logits for 7 classes (akiec, bcc, bkl, df, mel, nv, vasc)

preprocessing_techniques: Resizing images to a fixed size of 224x224x3 to match model input requirements
Data augmentation including random horizontal and vertical flips, random rotation, and color jittering to reduce overfitting and add variation
Normalization using ImageNet pretrained model mean [0.485, 0.456, 0.406] and standard deviation [0.229, 0.224, 0.225]
Conversion of images to PyTorch tensors

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Applied during data augmentation to reduce overfitting and add variation to the training dataset.

conclusions: The EfficientNetB3 standalone model and the EfficientNetB1 + ViT hybrid model achieved the highest accuracy of 0.89% on the HAM10000 dataset.

There was no significant performance improvement from the hybrid approach compared to the strong standalone EfficientNetB3 model, with tiny differences in accuracy (up to 0.04%), macro-averaged F1 score (up to 0.07%), and weighted-averaged F1 score (up to 0.04%).
EfficientNetB0 performed the worst among the non-hybrid models with an accuracy of 0.85%.
The EfficientNetB3 model is considered the strongest architecture for this specific task under the current configuration.
For the EfficientNetB1 + ViT hybrid model, Vascular Lesions and Dermatofibroma achieved perfect AUC scores, while Melanoma had the lowest AUC score of 0.95.
For the EfficientNetB3 model, Dermatofibroma achieved a perfect AUC score, Vascular Lesions had an AUC of 0.99, and Melanoma had the lowest AUC score of 0.94.
Further evaluation on more diverse data is needed to assess the robustness and generalization capability of the models.
Future work should include trials with different datasets and other models to improve the study.
important_remarks: The study relies exclusively on the HAM10000 dataset, which is known to have class imbalance and demographic biases (e.g., overrepresentation of certain skin tones and demographics), limiting the generalizability of the results to diverse real-world populations.

The paper acknowledges that deep learning models face challenges in real-world settings due to image artifacts (e.g., tattoos, colored markings) and variability in skin lesion presentations across different skin tones, which were not fully addressed in this controlled experiment.

The hybrid models (EfficientNet + ViT) showed no significant performance improvement over the standalone EfficientNetB3 model, with tiny differences in accuracy (up to 0.04%), macro-averaged F1 score (up to 0.07%), and weighted-averaged F1 score (up to 0.04%), suggesting limited benefit of the hybrid approach under the current configuration.

The study lacks external validation; the authors explicitly state that further evaluation on more diverse data and real-world datasets (including low-resolution or noisy inputs) is needed to assess robustness and clinical reliability.
Critical hyperparameters such as the optimizer, loss function, and training schedule are not reported, limiting the reproducibility of the study.
No statistical hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values, confidence intervals) was performed to determine if the minor performance differences between models are statistically significant.

The dataset was sourced from Kaggle, which may introduce selection bias compared to clinical cohorts, and the metadata (age, sex, localization) was not utilized in the model training, potentially missing important clinical context.

Melanoma, the most critical class for early detection, had the lowest AUC score (0.94 for EfficientNetB3 and 0.95 for EfficientNetB1+ViT) among all classes, indicating relative difficulty in distinguishing melanoma from other lesions compared to others like Dermatofibroma (perfect AUC).
